utterances: Are there people who actually go the opposite direction?
You mean people who are depressed in the summer?
Yeah.
Yes.
They're a smaller group of people and, mostly, you find those people in the South and, for some reason in Asian countries.
So there's a smaller - much smaller percent of seasonal depressives have summer, you know, depressions.
And now, I've heard of therapies that involve going to the beach instead.
Why would going to the beach work?
Well, for two reasons.
One, it's very sunny.
Yeah.
Two, it's very beautiful.
And three, it's filled with negative ions.
The beach is?
The beach is.
Yes.

Wet, damp places have an excess of negative charge and although it sounds like snake oil, actually there's good empirical evidence that negative ion generators can actually improve seasonal depression, just like phototherapy.
So you could get a negative ion generator?
You could get a high-flow negative ion generator.
Yeah.
I've heard about positive, negative ions for years, I've heard about...
Well, you know, the folklore about these hot, dry winds that drive people mad?
Uh-huh.
Well, there's actually some scientific basis behind it because these hot, dry winds like the Santa Ana and the Foehn and the Mistral are full of positive ions and they do drive people...
Because I've also heard that the way animals know that the weather is going to change or people - they start sniffing the air, that they're detecting, possibly, the ion change that's coming.
Yeah, possibly.
Also.
Is it easier to get the light box or the negative ion generator?
Which is it...
Probably easier at this point to get the light box, and maybe cheaper.
Is it?
Yeah.
I think so.
And you can build your own if you want.
You could, although the issue is, you want to really be sure that you've got a filter that will take away as much UV light as possible because you don't want to harm your eyes.
Right.
You know, people will say to you, why don't you just get out of bed and go jog someplace?
You'll feel a lot better.
Yes.
Well, people who don't experience depression always say things like that.
You pull yourself up by your own bootstraps.
It's very hard.
Yeah.
If you could get out of bed, you wouldn't be depressed.
By definition.
By definition.
Thank you very much for joining us today.
My pleasure.
We're talking to Richard Friedman, professor of psychiatry at Weill Cornell Medical College here in New York.
Good morning.
I'm Rachel Martin.
The woman believed to be the oldest person who ever lived maybe wasn't.
Two researchers now suggest Jeanne Calment of France may not have been 122 years old when she died in 1997.
Instead, they hypothesize it was actually her 99-year-old daughter who had assumed her identity after she died.
The French researcher who helped validate Calment's age, Jean-Marie Robine, says the new theory is false.
Do you have any idea how many people would have needed to lie?
It's MORNING EDITION.
Back now with DAY TO DAY.
I'm Robert Smith.
And I'm Madeleine Brand.
We in the media love to report science news, especially if it's health-related; you know, the story that starts off a new study by the such and such journal says doing X will make you live longer.
But there always seems to be another study a little later on that says the opposite, that X won't make you live longer.
Garlic, red wine, aspirin, all once touted as beneficial, now maybe not.
Well, why does that happen?
Here with an answer is writer Gary Taubes, whose cover story in this coming Sunday's New York Times Magazine is called ""Unhealthy Science."" And Gary Taubes is here now.
Welcome to DAY TO DAY.
Thank you.
It's nice to be here.
Well, the unhealthy science that you're referring to is epidemiology and the big epidemiological study that's in a lot of this health news comes from the Nurses Health Study.
Now, describe what that is.
In South Carolina, conservative Republicans rallied behind Gingrich at the last minute, undeterred by a week of tawdry revelations and negative campaign ads.
NPR's Debbie Elliott spoke with voters about what swayed them.
You're exactly right, Farai.
Look, I mean, everyone used this as a platform to advance their own personal opinions or feelings or whatever you want to call it as it relates to the war in Iraq and also in Afghanistan.
What I found very interesting about the questioning yesterday, Farai, is that not only was it civil, but also it was pretty intellectually challenging for the most part.
I mean, all the senators, even the ones that are not running for president, made their points and made them very, very clearly.
They strongly disagreed and/or agreed with the administration on particular policy.
But even more importantly, and I hate to say this, but I think I'm right here, is that General Petraeus was almost just like a prop on this massive stage.
And the reason why is because Senators McCain, Obama and Clinton were not necessarily speaking to General Petraeus.
They were actually speaking to the American people through General Petraeus to augment whatever they feel about the Iraq war.
Senator McCain made his points and made them very, very well, as well as Senators Obama and Clinton.
I actually thought Senator Clinton's comments were much less divisive as they were in the past.
Remember when General Petraeus was up on Capitol Hill in the summer time.
It was very bombastic.
It was very combative.

It was almost like, how dare you sit here and lie to me because the facts that you are portraying, General Petraeus, are not the facts that we're seeing on the television screens and that we're listening on NPR.
So yesterday was very, very interesting and it was a complete 360, if you will, as opposed to the line of questioning come, you know, six months ago.
Well, let's move on to some other presidential campaign news.
Hillary Clinton's chief campaign strategist Mark Penn stepped down over the weekend.
As part of his duties at a private firm, he was part of a meeting, scheduled meeting, with Colombian officials to discuss a free trade pact.
The problem was that Senator Clinton opposes that deal.
So, Donna, could this hurt Senator Clinton in the upcoming Pennsylvania primary because the whole issue of free trade and NAFTA has been such a thorn in the side of some voters?
I think so, only because it's a distraction.
Senator Clinton opposes the free trade pact with Colombia.
She has disagreements with the NAFTA trade deal, as well.
She was involved with her husband, of course, supported it back in the 1990s.
Former President Bill Clinton supports the Colombia Free Trade Act.
That, you know, that's also a little bit of a difficulty for her.
And Mark Penn is still involved in the campaign, not as chief strategist, but his company is still doing polling as well as doing the mail.
So I think you will find some of the labor unions taking issue with Senator Clinton for keeping Mark Penn on the payroll, so to speak, although it's not Mark Penn personally.
It's his firm.
His firm has been paid over 10 million dollars thus far in delivering various services to the Clinton campaign.
To the extent that he's still at the table, although not at the head of the table.
He becomes a campaign issue.
Robert, a friend of mine who is working on one of the campaigns said that your whole goal is to keep the campaign staff out of the news.
Is this hurtful mainly in the sense that Donna says of being a distraction from the message?
Yes.
I mean, look, this is just yet another hiccup in a long road of hiccups.
First, you had Patti Solis Doyle who was the first campaign manager for Senator Clinton.
You also had some surrogates out there on both sides on Senator Obama's side and also on Senator Clinton's side making some unfortunate remarks.
And now you have Mark Penn.
Look, the average person out there does not know who Mark Penn is nor do they care.
But what they do care about is the Colombian Free Trade Agreement.
They care about gas prices.
They care about healthcare.
They care about the direction of this country and, of course, they care about Iraq.
So I think we should put this into perspective that the average person out there probably doesn't care about Mark Penn, doesn't really care about whether he's on payroll or not.
But again, they care about the overall direction of Senator Clinton's campaign.
If in fact she is projecting this image, if you will, of someone that is looking out for their best interest, and again, this is something that is a hiccup.
Will it be the nail in the coffin for Senator Clinton's campaign?
Probably not.
But again, this is the last thing that she needs.
We're going to make a run at this later in the show on our Bloggers' Roundtable, but I want to give you guys a quick crack at Condoleezza Rice.
Senator McCain is putting together his short list of vice presidential candidates, and Condoleezza Rice has been variously portrayed as seeking it and not seeking it.
What do you think, Donna?
First of all, she's not seeking it.
She's not interested.
She has indicated on numerous occasions that she is returning to California, returning to Stanford, returning to academic life.
I think Condoleezza Rice is a little bit tired of Washington, D.C.
at this point in terms of when her service is over with at the end of President Bush's term, she hopes to return to private life again.
